title: Anthropic  Designs Three-Agent Harness Supports Long-Running Full-Stack AI Development
author: byLeela Kumili
summary: Anthropic has introduced a multi-agent harness architecture designed to support long-running autonomous AI development tasks, particularly for frontend design and full-stack software creation. The system decomposes work into three distinct agent roles: planning, generation, and evaluation. A key innovation is the separation of evaluation from generation—a dedicated evaluator agent with calibrated scoring criteria prevents generators from overrating their outputs, particularly on subjective tasks. The framework addresses critical challenges in extended AI workflows, including context loss and task termination, through structured handoff artifacts and context resets rather than context compaction. For frontend design specifically, the evaluator uses four grading criteria (design quality, originality, craft, and functionality) and interacts with live pages via Playwright MCP to provide detailed critiques guiding iterative refinement. Iterations typically range from 5-15 cycles per run, sometimes taking up to four hours, producing progressively refined designs. The structured approach enables better handling of multi-hour sessions by clearly defining agent responsibilities and dependencies, supporting both sequential and parallel execution. While some evaluation mechanisms require human oversight for initial calibration, the framework facilitates reproducibility and incremental progress. Industry feedback highlights that the key breakthrough is enforcing structure—JSON specs, testing, and commit-by-commit progress—to overcome context window limitations in long-running agents. As models improve, this architecture may evolve, with some tasks handled directly by next-generation models while harnesses tackle increasingly complex work.
content: InfoQ Homepage News Anthropic Designs Three-Agent Harness Supports Long-Running Full-Stack AI Development Architecture & Design Shipping Faster, Breaking More: Rethinking Delivery Systems in the Age of AI (Webinar May 28th) Anthropic Designs Three-Agent Harness Supports Long-Running Full-Stack AI Development Apr 04, 2026 2 min read by Leela Kumili Write for InfoQ Feed your curiosity. Help 550k+ global senior developers each month stay ahead. Get in touch Listen to this article - 0:00 Audio ready to play Your browser does not support the audio element. 0:00 0:00 Normal 1.25x 1.5x Like Reading list Anthropic has introduced a multi-agent harness design to support long-running autonomous application development , targeting both frontend design and full-stack software creation. The approach divides tasks among distinct agents responsible for planning, generation, and evaluation, aiming to maintain coherence and improve output quality over multi-hour AI sessions. The design addresses challenges common in autonomous coding workflows, such as context loss and premature task termination. Engineers at Anthropic implemented context resets alongside structured handoff artifacts, enabling the next agent in the workflow to continue from a defined state. This differs from compaction, which preserves context but can make models cautious about approaching context limits, potentially affecting performance in extended tasks. Self-evaluation of outputs was another focus. Agents often overrate their results, particularly on subjective tasks such as design. To mitigate this, Anthropic introduced a separate evaluator agent calibrated with few-shot examples and scoring criteria. Prithvi Rajasekaran , engineering lead at Anthropic Labs, says Separating the agent doing the work from the agent judging it proves to be a strong lever to address this issue. For frontend design, the team established four grading criteria: design quality, originality, craft, and functionality. The evaluator navigates live pages, interacts with the interface using Playwright MCP, and provides detailed critiques to guide the generator in iterative cycles. Each cycle produces progressively refined outputs. Iterations range from five to fifteen per run, sometimes taking up to four hours, and generate designs that combine visual distinction with functional accuracy. Industry practitioners have highlighted the framework’s structured approach. Artem Bredikhin wrote on LinkedIn that long-running AI agents fail for a simple reason: every new context window is amnesia. The breakthrough is structure: JSON feature specs, enforced testing, commit-by-commit progress, and an init script that ensures every session starts with a working app. Another observer, Raghus Arangarajan , commented on LinkedIn that The three-agent framework provides a repeatable workflow for multi-hour sessions and ensures that evaluation and iteration are separated from generation, improving overall reliability and output quality. Anthropic engineers applied this framework across different task types to assess performance improvements. They observed that separating planning, generation, and evaluation enables better handling of subjective assessments while maintaining reproducibility in objective tasks. The structured multi-agent workflow also facilitates incremental progress in long-running sessions by clearly defining responsibilities and handoffs between agents. Operationally, teams must establish evaluation criteria and calibrate scoring mechanisms while monitoring iterative output. Agents execute evaluations automatically, but human oversight remains important for initial calibration and quality validation. The workflow supports distributed processing of tasks and allows multiple agents to run in parallel or sequentially based on dependencies. As AI models improve, the harness’s role may shift, with some tasks handled directly by next-generation models. At the same time, improved models enable harnesses to tackle more complex work. Engineers should experiment, monitor traces, decompose tasks, and adjust harnesses, as the space of harness combinations evolves with model capabilities. title: 15 Agentic AI Design Patterns You Should Know Research Backed And Emerging Frameworks 2026
summary: This article presents 15 agentic AI design patterns for 2026, covering both established and emerging frameworks for building autonomous AI agents. The patterns appear to focus on practical architectures and design methodologies that developers can use to structure AI systems that can operate with minimal human intervention. These design patterns are research-backed and represent current best practices in the field of agentic AI development. For technical readers, understanding these patterns is crucial as agentic AI systems are becoming increasingly important for automation, autonomous decision-making, and complex problem-solving. The article serves as a comprehensive reference guide for architects and engineers looking to implement sophisticated AI agent systems in production environments. With AI agents becoming more prevalent across industries, knowledge of these design patterns helps developers make informed architectural decisions and avoid common pitfalls when building systems that require autonomous behavior, reasoning, and task execution.
source_url: AI agent harness and agentic design patterns

title: MASTERING AGENTIC AI DESIGN PATTERNS: THE COMPLETE DEVELOPER’S BLUEPRINT FOR BUILDING, SCALING, AND AUTOMATING INTELLIGENT MULTI-AGENT SYSTEMS WITH LANGGRAPH, AUTOGEN, AND MCP FRAMEWORKS: cordex, finn: 9798273485341: Amazon.com: Books
author: finn cordex(Author)
summary: This is a 2026 developer's guide to building intelligent multi-agent AI systems, authored by Finn Cordex, a senior AI engineer. The book covers mastering agentic AI design patterns using three key frameworks: LangGraph, AutoGen, and the Model Context Protocol (MCP). It progresses from foundational concepts of autonomous multi-agent architectures through practical design patterns including planner-executor, tool-chaining, reflection, and coordination strategies. The guide emphasizes production-ready implementations, with detailed coverage of LangGraph's graph-based orchestration for Python workflows, AutoGen's collaborative agent design with message-passing protocols, and MCP for cross-agent communication and interoperability. Technical readers will find value in the optimization techniques for scalability, error-handling strategies, and end-to-end Python implementations demonstrating real-world agentic pipelines. This resource is designed for engineers, data scientists, and enterprise developers seeking to move beyond experimental AI systems into robust, enterprise-grade intelligent automation solutions for copilots, AI workflow engines, and large-scale decision systems.

Using your mobile phone camera - scan the code below and download the Kindle app. Unlock the Next Era of Artificial Intelligence Engineering.Mastering Agentic AI Design Patterns (2026 Edition)is the definitive developer’s guide to building, scaling, and automating intelligent multi-agent systems usingLangGraph,AutoGen, and theModel Context Protocol (MCP)frameworks. Authored byFinn Cordex, a senior AI engineer and technical author with over a decade of hands-on experience in applied machine learning and large-scale LLM architectures, this book dives deep into the frameworks and design strategies powering today’s most advanced agentic systems. Agentic Foundations:Understand the evolution from traditional LLM pipelines to fully autonomous, multi-agent architectures. Design Pattern Blueprints:Learn reusable system patterns—planner-executor, tool-chaining, reflection, and coordination—that bring reliability and scalability to complex AI ecosystems. LangGraph in Practice:Build structured agentic workflows with LangGraph’s graph-based orchestration model and see how it integrates seamlessly with Python environments. AutoGen for Collaboration:Design conversational and task-sharing agents that coordinate naturally, leveraging AutoGen’s message-passing protocols and memory management. The MCP Framework:Implement cross-agent communication protocols for interoperability, modularity, and security in distributed AI systems. Optimization & Scalability:Apply advanced caching, error-handling, and adaptive planning techniques to deploy robust, production-grade AI solutions. End-to-End Examples:Explore complete, annotated Python implementations that demonstrate real-world agentic pipelines—from design to deployment. As agentic frameworks redefine how intelligent systems are built and automated, the ability to design and manage these architectures has become a critical skill for engineers, data scientists, and enterprise developers alike.Mastering Agentic AI Design Patterns (2026 Edition)delivers the technical clarity and implementation depth required to move beyond experimentation and into scalable, production-ready systems. Whether you are building custom copilots, AI workflow engines, or large-scale decision systems, this book provides theblueprint, tools, and mindsetto harness the full power of agentic AI. If you’re ready to master the design patterns driving the future of intelligent automation—this book is your gateway. title: Agentic Engineering Patterns
summary: This article explores agentic engineering patterns and design approaches for building AI agents. It discusses how to architect systems where autonomous agents can operate effectively, covering practical patterns for agent development, integration with external systems, and management of agent behavior. The content addresses the engineering challenges of implementing reliable, controllable agents in production environments, including considerations for safety, observability, and scalability. This is relevant for developers building AI systems who need to understand structural patterns and best practices for agent-based architectures.
